title: Data-driven analysis of metastability in a stochastic bistable system
authors: Ankan Banerjee, Manuel Santos Gutierrez, John Moroney, Valerio Lucarini
abstract: We study the metastability properties of a simple prototypical bistable system using the formalism of the Koopman operator. Instead of studying noise-induced transitions by following the trajectories of the system, we track them by studying the time evolution and the decay rate of the subdominant mode of the Koopman operator, thus in a geometry-agnostic framework. We find agreement with the predictions - both the exponential and subexponential ones - of large deviation theory in the weak-noise limit for the statistics of escape time, both in equilibrium and nonequilibrium conditions. The subdominant Koopman mode also allows for an accurate reconstruction of the competing basins of attraction. Going deeper in the Koopman spectrum, we are able to recognise modes that are associated with intrawell variability as well as with the escape of trajectories from the saddle towards the attractor, both in the equilibrium and nonequilibrium case. Our methodology, being grounded in purely data-driven techniques, could be helpful for studying high-dimensional metastable systems.
summary: This paper presents a data-driven approach using Koopman operator theory to identify metastable states and quantify transition rates in stochastic systems. It demonstrates that the subdominant Koopman mode can reconstruct basin boundaries and that its decay rate accurately predicts escape times. The method is robust for both equilibrium and non-equilibrium dynamics, even in the presence of rotational forces.
main_problem: Developing a purely data-driven, geometry-agnostic method to study noise-induced transitions and metastability in complex stochastic systems where governing equations are unknown.
main_result: The Koopman operator framework accurately reconstructs basin boundaries and predicts escape time statistics, showing strong agreement with Large Deviation Theory in both equilibrium and non-equilibrium regimes.
method: The authors use Extended Dynamic Mode Decomposition (EDMD) to approximate the Koopman and Perron-Frobenius operators from time-series data, validated against Ulam's method and analytical Large Deviation Theory.
model_or_system: A 2D stochastic bistable system (Rotating Double Well Potential) described by an Ito diffusion process, featuring two attractors, a saddle point, and a rotational component that drives the system out of equilibrium.
key_observables: Mean exit time, subdominant Koopman modes (for basin reconstruction), Koopman eigenvalues (decay rates), and the 0-level set of the subdominant mode.
important_parameters: Noise intensity (alpha), rotation strength (mu), and the weak-noise limit (alpha -> 0).
main_assumptions: The noise is assumed to be non-degenerate (invertible covariance), and the dictionary of basis functions must sufficiently span the relevant subspace.
figures_summary: Plots of Koopman eigenvalues and spectral gaps; heat maps of Koopman modes overlaid with true basin boundaries; time-series visualizations of mode-based transition identification; and eigenfunctions of the Fokker-Planck operator.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of noise-induced tipping, presents the Koopman-based data-driven framework, applies it to a 2D rotating bistable model, validates results against Large Deviation Theory, and explores the role of saddle dynamics and rotation.
why_interesting: The method provides a way to characterize transitions and metastable states in high-dimensional, non-equilibrium systems using only data, which is highly relevant for studying dissipation and decoherence in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Eigenvalues (λj = log (σj)/ft) of the generator of the semigroup of Koopman operators for different strengths of rotation (µ = 0, 1, 5, 10) corresponding to the noise inten- sity α = 0.3, where σjs are eigenvalues of the approximated Koopman matrix (K) and ft = 0.005. (b) The absolute differ- ence between the real part of first two eigenvalues are shown for values of µ used above. The plot infers that the spectral gap increases with µ.

FIG. 3. A visual depiction of our method for identifying a transition from one attractor to another. Time series showed here are corresponding to µ = 0, 1, 5 and 10, and α = 0.3. (Top) - time series of observables; (middle) - the first sub- dominant Koopman mode φ2, evaluated on x(t) , and y(t), density of the time series is also shown on the right of each plots; (bottom) - time series of normalized-φ2. Dashed green curves represent basin boundaries at φ2 = 0. A crossing oc- curs when the normalized φ2 crosses the basin boundary.
FIG. 2. First subdominant mode of the Koopman operator (φ2, normalised between -1 and 1) for different values of µ and α.

FIG. 4. Test of the linear relationship between the logarithm of ⟨τα⟩and 1α2 for different values of µ. Filled green cir- cles with errorbars (indicating the 95% confidence interval), are the estimation of mean exit times, as computed from en- sembles. Dashed black lines are linear fit of these estimators. Shaded regions denote 95% confidence interval of the fit. Solid orange lines are theoretical estimation of exit times - half of mean transition times. Inverse of the modulus of second eigen value of the Koopman operator estimated by EDMD (solid red triangles), inverse of the second eigenvalue of the Markov matrix computed using the Ulam’s method (hollow inverted blue triangles) are also...

FIG. 5. Crossing points (blue and red crosses) for different noise strengths α and rotation rate µ are plotted together with contours of φ2. The blue circle, the red circle, and the green hollow star represent two attractors and the saddle point, re- spectively. Red and blue colour shadings correspond to dif- ferent level sets. The green line is the 0-line, which defines the basin boundary.
